articles.title: The Reshoring Bill Is Coming Due — and the Freight Market Isn't Ready
articles.excerpt: Micron is spending $250 billion to build chips in America. Toyota is moving Tacoma production to Texas. Apple and Broadcom are pledging billions more. Reshoring is no longer a policy aspiration — it's a construction project. But the freight market underpinning all of it is already running out of room.

articles.title: The Automation Stack Is Consolidating, and the Winners Won't Be Who You Think
articles.excerpt: Symbotic just bought a UK orchestration software company. Luxonis raised a Series A for AI vision. Humanoid robots are claiming near-human dexterity. Taken separately, these are just industry news. Taken together, they're a map of where the automation stack is heading — and the consolidation logic is clearer than it looks.

articles.title: The Suez Return Is Not a Recovery — It's a Reset
articles.excerpt: Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd just announced the first Gemini service will return to the Suez Canal route. Most people will read this as good news. I think it's more complicated than that.

articles.title: Truck Rates Are Up 31% and Nobody Seems to Know What to Do About It
articles.excerpt: Spot truck rates jumped 31% year-over-year in May. That number deserves more attention than it's getting — because the pressures driving it aren't going away, and they're about to collide with a wave of reshoring that assumes cheap domestic freight.

articles.title: Pax Silica and the Quiet Rewiring of Industrial Supply Chains
articles.excerpt: The EU just signed onto the US-led Pax Silica initiative, committing to shared rare earth and semiconductor supply chains across trusted allies. Most coverage treated it as a tech policy story. It isn't.

articles.title: The Onshoring Premium: What Lovesac's Sactionals Move Tells Us About the Real Cost of Tariff Strategy
articles.excerpt: Lovesac is moving production of its core sofa line to the US this summer. The product will be different from the version made in Vietnam. That detail is more important than the announcement itself.

articles.title: Humanoid Robots Are Reaching the Factory Floor. The Bottleneck Was Never the Hardware.
articles.excerpt: BMW just deployed Figure 03 at scale. Agility Robotics is going public. AGIBOT hit 15,000 units. The hardware is arriving faster than expected — but I think the industry is about to discover that deploying a robot and integrating a robot are two very different problems.

articles.title: The Logistics Stack Is Being Rebuilt From the Top Down, and CMA CGM Is Writing the Blueprint
articles.excerpt: CMA CGM just bought FedEx's contract logistics unit for $1.4 billion. That's not a port operator diversifying. That's a company assembling a vertically integrated logistics stack — and it's further along than most people realize.

articles.title: The Reshored Factory Has No Warehouse: Why the Automation Industry Is Selling to the Wrong Buyer
articles.excerpt: US manufacturing is growing at its fastest rate since 2021 while cutting jobs — and the automation industry is celebrating the wrong part of that signal. The buyer who needs them most doesn't know it yet.

articles.title: Your Warehouse Contract Has a Data Clause Problem. You Probably Haven't Read It.
articles.excerpt: The Allbirds-to-Smartbird pivot isn't a retail story. It's an early signal that operational data generated inside warehouses and fulfillment systems is becoming a separable, monetizable asset — and most automation contracts written today say nothing coherent about who owns it.

articles.title: The Automation Pitch Is Wrong: Why Geopolitical Instability Is a Better ROI Argument Than Efficiency
articles.excerpt: Automation vendors are still leading with cost-per-pick. But the Hormuz disruption has quietly changed the decision calculus — and the companies that understand this will close deals the others can't explain why they're losing.
